Assuming the Wright-Fisher model, each of the 2N sampled gametes has probability $ i/(2N) $ of being B and probability $ [1-(i/2N)] $ of being b, yielding $$ P_{ij}=\binom{2N}{j}(i/2N)^{j}[1-(i/2N)]^{2N-j} $$ (2.1) where the first term in large parentheses is the binomial coefficient. Under such conditions, $$ E(r^{2})\simeq\frac{10+\rho}{22+13\rho+\rho^{2}} $$ (2.29b) which asymptotically approaches $ 1/\rho $ for large $ \rho $. Additional theoretical points of interest were developed in Strobeck and Morgan (1978) and Golding and Strobeck (1980). A more daunting

formulas.label: Formula 2.30a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: he number of different alleles in a sample. Insight into this quantity under drift-mutation equilibrium is given the probability of having $ k $ different alleles in a sample of $ n $ genes, which is $$ \begin{align*}\Pr(k\mid\theta_L,n)=\frac{S_n^k\;\theta_L^k}{S_n(\theta_L)}\end{align*} $$ (2.30a) $$ S_{n}(\theta_{L})=\theta_{L}(\theta_{L}+1)(\theta_{L}+2)\cdots(\theta_{L}+n-1) $$ (2.30b) where and $ S_n^k $ is the coefficient on the $ \theta_L^k $ term obtained by expanding the polynomial in

formulas.label: Formula 2.30b
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: um is given the probability of having $ k $ different alleles in a sample of $ n $ genes, which is $$ \begin{align*}\Pr(k\mid\theta_L,n)=\frac{S_n^k\;\theta_L^k}{S_n(\theta_L)}\end{align*} $$ (2.30a) $$ S_{n}(\theta_{L})=\theta_{L}(\theta_{L}+1)(\theta_{L}+2)\cdots(\theta_{L}+n-1) $$ (2.30b) where and $ S_n^k $ is the coefficient on the $ \theta_L^k $ term obtained by expanding the polynomial in Equation 2.30b (Ewens 1972). This formula opened up the field of formal statistical tests for

formulas.label: Formula 2.31a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: Several useful results follow from Equation 2.30a. First, the probability of a monomorphic sample is $$ \Pr(k=1)=\frac{(n-1)!}{(\theta_{L}+1)(\theta_{L}+2)\cdots(\theta_{L}+n-1)} $$ (2.31a)

formulas.label: Formula 2.31b
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: Second, a bit of algebra gives the mean and variance for the number of alleles in a sample as $$ \begin{align*}E(k)=1+\theta_L\cdot\sum\limits_{j=2}^n\frac{1}{\theta_L+j-1},\qquad\sigma^2(k)=\theta_L\cdot\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n-1}\frac{j}{(\theta_L+j)^2}\end{align*} $$ (2.31b)

formulas.label: Formula 2.32
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: , one allele appears as a singleton, whereas the other two alleles both appear as two copies, giving $ n_1 = 1 $, $ n_2 = 2 $, and $ n_3 $, $ n_4 $, $ n_5 = 0 $. The constraint on the $ n_i $ is that $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n}i\cdot n_{i}=n $$ (2.32)

formulas.label: Formula 2.33a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: y powerful result, due to Ewens (1972) and Karlin and McGregor (1972), is that the joint probability distribution of $ n_{1}, \cdots, n_{n} $ and k is given by the Ewens sampling formula (Ewens 1972) $$ \Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\cdots,n_{n},k\mid n)=\frac{n!\theta_{L}^{k}}{S_{n}(\theta_{L})\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\cdots n^{n_{n}}\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\cdots n_{n}!} $$ (2.33a)

formulas.label: Formula 2.33b
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: that the sample is monomorphic, Equation 2.31a, directly follows by setting $ k = 1, n_1 = n $. Equations 2.30a and 2.33a show that the conditional distribution of $ n_1, \cdots, n_n $ given $ k $ is $$ \Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\cdots,n_{n}\mid n,\ k)=\frac{n!}{S_{n}^{k}\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\cdots n^{n_{n}}\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\cdots n_{n}!} $$ (2.33b)

formulas.label: Formula 2.34a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: and mutation-drift equilibrium), the fraction of sites in the entire population that have a derived nucleotide at frequency x (for 0 < x < 1) is given by the (unfolded) Watterson (1975) distribution, $$ \phi(x)=\frac{\theta}{x}\quad for\quad\frac{1}{2N}\leq x\leq1-\frac{1}{2N} $$ (2.34a)

formulas.label: Formula 2.34b
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: derived nucleotides in the population. Reconfiguring Equation 2.34a for unpolarized alleles, so that $ 0 < x \leq 0.5 $ where x is the minor-allele frequency, gives the folded Watterson distribution, $$ \phi(x)=\frac{\theta}{x}+\frac{\theta}{1-x}=\frac{\theta}{x(1-x)}\quad for\quad\frac{1}{2N}\leq x\leq1/2 $$ (2.34b)

formulas.label: Formula 2.35a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
formulas.context_text: ows from the Watterson distribution. In the case of an unfolded frequency spectrum, for a sample of size n, the number $ s_{i} $ of the L total sites with i derived nucleotides has the expected value $$ E(s_{i})=\frac{\theta_{L}}{i},\quad for\quad1\leq i\leq n-1 $$ (2.35a)

formulas.label: Formula 2.35b
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION

formulas.context_text: opulation size, and absence of selection) can be tested (Chapter 9). Similarly, for a folded frequency spectrum, where $ s_i $ now denotes the number of sites with minor-nucleotide frequency $ i/n $, $$ E(s_{i})=\frac{\theta_{L}}{i}+\frac{\theta_{L}}{n-i}=\frac{\theta_{L}n}{i(n-i)},\quad for\quad1\leq i\leq[n/2] $$ (2.35b) where $ [n/2] $ denotes the largest integer $ \leq n/2 $.

formulas.label: Formula 2.36a
formulas.section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
formulas.subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum

formulas.label: Formula 2.36b

This formula, which forms the null model for several of the likelihood-based tests for a selective sweep (Chapters 8 and 9), is the infinite-sites anal

formulas.label: Formula 2.37
formulas.section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
formulas.context_text: $\lambda_1(1/2N)$ as the unconditional probability of coalescence two generations back. This simple rule can be generalized to give the probability of coalescence exactly $t$ generations in the past, $$ P_{c}(t)=\lambda_{1}^{t-1}(1/2N) $$ (2.37) which defines a geometric distribution, with the sum of $ P_c(t) $ over the interval $ t = 1 $ to $ \infty $ being equal to one. One simple related point is that the probability that the most recent

formulas.label: Formula 2.38
formulas.section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
formulas.context_text: The mean coalescence time for two randomly sampled genes is simply $$ \bar{t}_{c}(2)=\sum_{t=1}^{\infty}t\cdot P_{c}(t)=2N $$ (2.38)

formulas.label: Formula 2.39
formulas.section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
formulas.context_text: cence for any pair in the sample in the preceding generation is simply the product $p_n/(2N)$. Thus, the probability distribution for the coalescence time of one pair within a set of $n$ sequences is $$ P_{c}(p_{n},t)=[1-(p_{n}/2N)]^{t-1}[p_{n}/(2N)] $$ (2.39)

formulas.label: Formula 2.40
formulas.section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
formulas.context_text: $ (p_n=1) $, which again has an expected coalescence time of 2N generations (Figure 2.10). The implication of these results is that the expected time for merging n random lineages into n-1 lineages, $$ \bar{t}_{n}=2N/p_{n}=\frac{4N}{n(n-1)} $$ (2.40) increases with decreasing sample size.

formulas.label: Formula 2.41
formulas.section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
formulas.context_text: The total expected genealogical depth of a sample, obtained by summing the expectations of each coalescence event, is $$ \begin{align*}\bar t_c(n)=\sum_{i=2}^n{4N\over i(i-1)}=4N\left(1-{1\over n}\right)\end{align*} $$ (2.41)

formulas.label: Formula 2.42

formulas.subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: total metapopulation is just $ p_0(1 - p_0) $. $ F_{ST} $ is then defined as the fraction of the total variance attributable to the variance in the frequency of allele B among demes, $ \sigma^2(p) $, $$ F_{ST}=\frac{\sigma^{2}(p)}{p_{0}(1-p_{0})} $$ (2.42)

formulas.label: Formula 2.43
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: e expected variance in the allele frequency between two populations separated from a common ancestor t generations in the past (ignoring mutation and assuming no migration among groups), we have that $$ F_{ST}=\left[1-\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\right)^{t}\right]\simeq\frac{t}{2N_{e}}\quad for\quad t\ll N_{e} $$ (2.43)

formulas.label: Formula 2.44
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: at $ F_{ST} $ values are closely related to the average coalescence times of alleles within subpopulations, $ \bar{t}_{0} $, and of alleles drawn randomly from the entire metapopulation, $ \bar{t} $, $$ F_{S T}=\frac{\overline{t}-\overline{t}_{0}}{\overline{t}}=1-\frac{\overline{t}_{0}}{\overline{t}} $$ (2.44)

formulas.label: Formula 2.45a
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: 1971; Nagylaki 1982). Provided there is some potential migratory route between all demes, regardless of the level of migration, the mean coalescence time between random pairs of genes within demes is $$ \bar{t}_{0}=2N d $$ (2.45a) generations, i.e., twice the sum of the demic population sizes (Li 1976; Strobeck 1987; Hey 1991; Nagylaki 2000). Under this model, the independence of the within-deme variation of the migration rate

formulas.label: Formula 2.45b
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: On the other hand, the mean coalescence time for two genes randomly drawn from the entire metapopulation is $$ \begin{align*}\overline{t}=2Nd+{(d-1)^2\over2dm}\end{align*} $$ (2.45b)

formulas.label: Formula 2.46
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: les to coalesce into the same deme. Unlike the amount of variation within demes, the differentiation among demes clearly depends on m. Substituting Equations 2.45a and 2.45b into Equation 2.44 yields $$ F_{ST}=\frac{1}{1+4N\frac{md^{2}}{(1-d)^{2}}}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Nm}\quad for\quad d\gg1 $$ (2.46)

formulas.label: Formula 2.47a
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: the partitioning of the total population variation: $ F_{DG} $ among demes within groups, $ F_{GT} $ among groups within the total population, and $ F_{ST} $ among demes within the total population: $$ \begin{aligned}F_{DG}&=\frac{\bar{t}_{1}-\bar{t}_{0}}{\bar{t}_{1}}\\F_{GT}&=\frac{\bar{t}_{2}-\bar{t}_{1}}{\bar{t}_{2}}\\F_{ST}&=\frac{\bar{t}_{2}-\bar{t}_{0}}{\bar{t}_{2}}\end{aligned} $$ (2.47a)

formulas.label: Formula 2.47b
formulas.section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
formulas.subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
formulas.context_text: et al. 2009b). Substituting in the values for the various mean coalescence times noted previously, and assuming $d$ and $g$ moderately large and $m_{2} \ll m_{1}$, results in further simplification, $$ \begin{aligned}&F_{DG}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Nm_{1}}\\&F_{GT}\simeq F_{ST}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Ndm_{2}}\\ \end{aligned} $$ (2.47b)
